title: Biology
intro: Biology questions in this unit draw most heavily on molecular and cell biology: the machinery of DNA replication, transcription, and translation; the organelles and processes that keep a eukaryotic cell alive; and the laboratory techniques (PCR, gel electrophoresis, CRISPR, sequencing, blotting) used to study them. Biochemistry and metabolism form a closely related cluster, covering amino acids, enzymes, and the central pathways of glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, and photosynthesis.

Human physiology is the single largest content area, spanning every organ system: nervous (neurons, neurotransmitters, brain structures), endocrine (hormones and glands), circulatory, digestive, respiratory, renal, and reproductive. Associated diseases and disorders, from Alzheimer's and Parkinson's to genetic conditions like cystic fibrosis and sickle cell anemia, are frequently asked alongside the normal anatomy and physiology they disrupt. Immunology, covering antibodies, immune cell types, and autoimmune and infectious disease, is closely intertwined with both physiology and microbiology.

Genetics and evolutionary biology form another major cluster, ranging from Mendelian inheritance and population genetics to natural selection, speciation, and molecular evolution, frequently illustrated through model organisms such as Drosophila, E. coli, C. elegans, and Arabidopsis. Microbiology and virology cover bacteria, archaea, fungi, protists, and viruses, including the diseases they cause and the techniques (Gram staining, culturing) used to study them. Plant biology and ecology appear as smaller but consistent clusters, the former centered on plant anatomy and hormones, the latter on population and community-level interactions.

A modest set of questions asks about non-human animal diversity and behavior, and about the scientists and landmark experiments that built the field, including Mendel, Darwin, Watson and Crick, McClintock, and the Meselson-Stahl and Miller-Urey experiments. Across all these areas, the canon is dominated by a long tail of frequently recurring single terms rather than a small number of dominant answers, reflecting the breadth of introductory and molecular biology as tested subject matter.

sections.name: Evolutionary Biology
sections.blurb: Mechanisms and evidence of evolution, from natural selection and speciation to phylogenetics and molecular evolution.

entries.note: Measure of an organism's reproductive success and genetic contribution to the next generation.
Process by which populations evolve into distinct species, via allopatric, sympatric, or other mechanisms.
Random change in allele frequencies, most pronounced in small populations, as in founder effects and bottlenecks.
Diagrams depicting evolutionary relationships among taxa, built from morphological or molecular data.
Evolutionary force favoring traits that increase mating success, driving phenomena like the handicap principle.
Evolutionary strategy where species resemble others, Batesian, or share warning signals, Mullerian, to deter predators.
Idea that species must constantly adapt to survive against co-evolving competitors and parasites.
Behavior benefiting others at a cost to self, explained by kin selection and Hamilton's rule.
Permanent loss of a species, occurring at background rates or in mass extinction events.
Evolution of altruistic behavior favoring relatives' reproductive success, formalized by Hamilton's rule.
Independent evolution of similar traits in unrelated lineages facing similar selective pressures.
Darwin's mechanism by which heritable traits improving survival and reproduction become more common over generations.
Geologic period marked by rapid diversification of animal phyla, the "Cambrian explosion."
Method estimating divergence times between species using the rate of molecular mutations.

Rapid diversification of a lineage into new forms filling different ecological niches.

State in which an allele reaches 100% frequency in a population.

entries.note: Principle stating the rate of fitness increase equals the genetic variance in fitness.

Strategy of diversifying traits or offspring to reduce risk in unpredictable environments.

Reproductive strategy of reproducing once in a lifetime before dying, as in Pacific salmon.
Fish species widely studied as a model for rapid adaptive evolution.
Extinct marine arthropods abundant in the Paleozoic fossil record.

sections.name: Ecology
sections.blurb: Populations, communities, and ecosystems, including symbiosis, competition, and conservation concepts.

entries.note: Variety of species, genes, and ecosystems in a given area; measured by indices like species richness and evenness.
Interaction where organisms vie for limited resources, shaping niche differentiation and community structure.

Trait shaped by evolutionary pressures affecting metabolism, lifespan, and ecological role.

Functional role and set of conditions a species occupies within its environment.

Disturbance event that resets ecological succession and shapes fire-adapted ecosystems.

Reproductive strategy favoring few offspring with high parental investment, suited to stable environments.

sections.name: Microorganisms & Fungi
sections.blurb: Bacteria, archaea, protists, fungi, and viruses, along with the infectious diseases and lab techniques built around them.

sections.name: Immunology
sections.blurb: The cells, molecules, and diseases of the immune system, from antibodies to autoimmune disorders.

sections.name: Human Physiology & Anatomy
